text: The surgeon demonstrates the intracorporeal anastomosis technique for a robotic right colectomy, ensuring the cut edge of the ileum mesentery is straight. The ileum is positioned to form a gentle curve, aligning next to the transverse colon.

The surgeon uses the robotic third arm to provide counter traction on the bowel, facilitating the creation of an enterotomy on the anti-mesenteric side. Cautery is used to lift and cut into the bowel, ensuring a full-thickness incision into the intestinal wall, maintaining a distance of at least two centimeters from the staple line.

The surgeon uses the third arm of the robotic system to provide counter-traction on the transverse colon, facilitating the opening of the colon away from the tinea coli. The colon is opened sharply using cautery, similar to the previous technique used on the bowel.

The surgeon uses scissors to cut into the bowel wall, with one blade inside and one blade outside, lifting up to make the incision. The stapler is then inserted into the ileum, demonstrating the ease of positioning the mobile ileum against the immobile transverse colon for anastomosis.

The surgeon positions the ilium and brings the stapler into the colon, achieving precise alignment. The stapler is fired, and the third arm is used to retract the apex of the staple line upwards, ensuring the edges are aligned. The common channel is visible, and the staple line shows no bleeding, with the crotch of the staple line widely exposed.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-lock PDS suture on a V-twin needle to close the bottom crotch of the anastomosis, taking smaller bites to ensure a secure closure and prevent leaks. The needle tip falls off, prompting the use of a new needle and suture.

The surgeon uses the third arm to retract the apex of the staple line, aligning the edges of the bowel efficiently. Full thickness bites are taken along the suture line, which is exposed clearly as the apex is retracted posteriorly, ensuring precise closure.

The surgeon takes large bites to roll the last portion of the bowel tip, finishing the closure by running the suture from the apex downwards. Hardy, seromuscular bites are taken on both sides of the suture line to imbricate the bowel wall, resembling a pseudo-limber type suture technique.

The surgeon uses a long, twelve-inch relock suture to roll the suture line over itself, providing an extra layer of protection. The edges of the bowel visibly roll in on themselves as the surgeon works down to the previously closed bottom crotch, taking thick, full-thickness bites to secure the closure.

The surgeon places a crotch stitch at the point of maximal tension in the staple line to prevent unrolling, taking a thick bite at the edge of the anastomosis. The staple line crotch is visible below, resulting in a complete closure and a nicely completed anastomosis.
